front: Film: Hynkel (dictator of Tomainia) dances with an inflatable globe set to the Lohengrin prelude; a Jewish barber is mistaken for him
Film: Lohengrin prelude (Wagner) was chosen because both the director and Hitler greatly admired Wagner
Film: the director watched Triumph of the Will at MoMA and used it as source material to mock Hitler's mannerisms
Film: director reflected that he was 'the comic' while a man born four days after him in April 1889 was 'the madman'
Film: the Tramp raises money to restore a blind flower girl's sight; she had mistaken him for a millionaire
Film: score uses the habanera 'La Violetera' as a leitmotif for a blind florist; arranged by Alfred Newman
Film: contains a long boxing sequence where the protagonist hides behind a tall referee opposite Hank Mann
Film: factory worker is tested by a feeding machine, then trapped in machine gears, then jailed
Film: the Tramp is arrested for accidentally waving a red flag that fell from a truck, mistaken for a Communist protest leader
Film: love theme 'Smile' was inspired by the first-act love duet from Tosca; co-composed with David Raksin
Film: 'Smile' was Michael Jackson's favorite song; the film stars Paulette Goddard
Film: the Tramp eats a boiled shoe in a wobbling cabin blown off a cliff by a blizzard
Film: dream sequence shows the protagonist imagining a Dreamland of angels invaded by devils who conjure a woman to seduce him as he strums a harp
Film: uncharacteristically plays a Parisian bank teller who marries and murders rich wives; James Agee wrote a three-part defense of it
Director: won only competitive Oscar at age 84 for 'Terry's Theme' / 'Eternally' from Limelight (re-released 1973)
Director: son of an alcoholic father and asylum patient mother; supported her by clog dancing as a child; uncle of Jefferson Airplane drummer Spencer Dryden
Director: J. Edgar Hoover orchestrated a smear campaign, including trumped-up Mann Act charges from his relationship with Joan Barry
Director: married Oona O'Neill in 1943, causing Eugene O'Neill to disown his daughter over the age gap
Director: Little Tramp persona inspired Raj Kapoor's character in Awaara and Shree 420
Director: the Lettrist schism began when Guy Debord disrupted one of his press conferences
Director: Hannah Arendt argued he embodied 'the Jewish pariah mentality' in artistic form despite his non-Jewish heritage
Director: Hart Crane wrote a poem titled 'Chaplinesque'; Carl Sandburg wrote 'Without the Cane & Derby (For C.C.)'
Director: borrowed gags from and helped develop gags for French comedic icon Max Linder (referenced in Inglourious Basterds)
Director: called Cantinflas 'the greatest comedian alive'; asked Upton Sinclair to finance Eisenstein's unfinished Mexico film
